fig01: Grouped bar chart comparing four methods across five benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K, MATH, GPQA). Each bar shows mean accuracy (\%) averaged over 5 seeds; error bars denote sample standard deviation. \textbf{OursModel} (green, bold outline) achieves the highest accuracy on every benchmark, with gains of 3.3--5.8 pp over the next-best Method-B. Hatching distinguishes methods for grayscale printing.
fig02: Validation loss over 50 training steps for three methods (mean $\pm$ 95\% CI across $n{=}5$ seeds, log-scale $y$-axis). \textbf{OursModel} converges fastest and achieves the lowest final loss; the double-headed arrow marks the gap $\Delta{=}0.155$ between OursModel and the next-best method (Method-A) at step~49.
fig03:
%
  Chinchilla-style neural scaling law across 80 models.
  Each point is one model; axes are $\log_{10}(\text{parameters})$ vs.\
  $\log_{10}(\text{validation loss})$.
  The red line is the OLS fit
  ($y = -0.0449\,x + 0.621$, $R^2 = 0.877$),
  consistent with the Chinchilla power-law regime.
  The orange marker (``OursModel'') highlights the closest data
  point to the target coordinates $(9.4,\,0.205)$, identified as
  $(\log_{10}N{=}9.449,\;\text{loss}{=}0.210)$.
fig04:
%
  \textbf{(A)} Conceptual overview of the three-stage pipeline (Encode $\to$ Attend+Mem $\to$ Decode) with a residual-and-skill bypass connection.
  \textbf{(B)} Ablation study: removing attention (\texttt{-attn}), memory (\texttt{-mem}), residual (\texttt{-residual}), or skill (\texttt{-skill}) components each degrades the score relative to the full model (64.7).
  \textbf{(C)} Training loss curves over 30k steps; our method converges faster and to a lower final loss than the baseline.
  \textbf{(D)} Sensitivity sweep over hyperparameter axes $S$ and $P$; cell values indicate performance (higher is better, scale 0--1).%
